topics.title: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)
topics.definition: PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) is a framework of people, policies, hardware, software, and procedures that manages digital certificates and public-key cryptography. It establishes trust between communicating parties.
examPoints: PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) is a framework of people, policies, hardware, software, and procedures that manages digital certificates and public-key cryptography. It establishes trust between communicating parties.
Provides: Authentication, Encryption, Digital Signatures, Certificate Management
topics.narration:
Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)

PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) is a framework of people, policies, hardware, software, and procedures that manages digital certificates and public-key cryptography. It establishes trust between communicating parties.

Important exam points. PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) is a framework of people, policies, hardware, software, and procedures that manages digital certificates and public-key cryptography. It establishes trust between communicating parties.. PKI = Trust Management System. Provides: Authentication, Encryption, Digital Signatures, Certificate Management

topics.title: Why PKI is Required
topics.definition: PKI solves the problem of securely verifying identities over untrusted networks. It binds a public key to a verified identity using a digital certificate.
examPoints: PKI solves the problem of securely verifying identities over untrusted networks. It binds a public key to a verified identity using a digital certificate.
Anyone can claim any public key.
topics.narration:
Why PKI is Required

PKI solves the problem of securely verifying identities over untrusted networks. It binds a public key to a verified identity using a digital certificate.

Important exam points. PKI solves the problem of securely verifying identities over untrusted networks. It binds a public key to a verified identity using a digital certificate.. Without PKI:. Anyone can claim any public key.. No trusted identity verification.

topics.title: Problems Solved by PKI
topics.narration:
Problems Solved by PKI

Important exam points. Secure Key Distribution. Digital Signatures. Certificate Management. Secure TLS/HTTPS. Identity Verification

topics.title: Goals of PKI
topics.definition: The primary goal of PKI is to establish trusted communication between entities using digital certificates.
examPoints: The primary goal of PKI is to establish trusted communication between entities using digital certificates.
topics.narration:
Goals of PKI

The primary goal of PKI is to establish trusted communication between entities using digital certificates.

Important exam points. The primary goal of PKI is to establish trusted communication between entities using digital certificates.. Provides:. Authentication. Confidentiality. Integrity. Non-Repudiation

topics.title: PKI Architecture
topics.definition: PKI architecture consists of trusted components that issue, manage, validate, distribute, and revoke digital certificates.
examPoints: PKI architecture consists of trusted components that issue, manage, validate, distribute, and revoke digital certificates.
topics.narration:
PKI Architecture

PKI architecture consists of trusted components that issue, manage, validate, distribute, and revoke digital certificates.

Important exam points. PKI architecture consists of trusted components that issue, manage, validate, distribute, and revoke digital certificates.. Main Components:. CA. RA. Repository. VA. Subscriber. Relying Party

topics.title: PKI Components
topics.definition: PKI components work together to issue, validate, distribute, and manage digital certificates.
examPoints: PKI components work together to issue, validate, distribute, and manage digital certificates.
workflows: CA RA Repository VA Subscriber Relying Party
topics.narration:
PKI Components

PKI components work together to issue, validate, distribute, and manage digital certificates.

Important exam points. PKI components work together to issue, validate, distribute, and manage digital certificates.. Remember:

Workflow. CA RA Repository VA Subscriber Relying Party

topics.title: Certificate Authority (CA)
topics.definition: A Certificate Authority (CA) is the trusted organization responsible for issuing, digitally signing, renewing, and revoking digital certificates.
examPoints: A Certificate Authority (CA) is the trusted organization responsible for issuing, digitally signing, renewing, and revoking digital certificates.
topics.narration:
Certificate Authority (CA)

A Certificate Authority (CA) is the trusted organization responsible for issuing, digitally signing, renewing, and revoking digital certificates.

Important exam points. A Certificate Authority (CA) is the trusted organization responsible for issuing, digitally signing, renewing, and revoking digital certificates.. Responsibilities:. Issue Certificates. Sign Certificates. Publish CRLs. Manage PKI Trust

topics.title: Why CA is Trusted
topics.definition: The CA is trusted because its Root Certificate is installed in trusted root stores maintained by operating systems and browsers.
examPoints: The CA is trusted because its Root Certificate is installed in trusted root stores maintained by operating systems and browsers.
topics.narration:
Why CA is Trusted

The CA is trusted because its Root Certificate is installed in trusted root stores maintained by operating systems and browsers.

Important exam points. The CA is trusted because its Root Certificate is installed in trusted root stores maintained by operating systems and browsers.. Trust comes from:. Trusted Root Store. Digital Signature

topics.title: Types of Certificate Authorities
topics.narration:
Types of Certificate Authorities

Important exam points. Intermediate CA (Sub CA). Issuing CA

topics.title: Root CA
topics.definition: The Root CA is the highest authority in the PKI hierarchy. It is self-signed and signs Intermediate CA certificates.
examPoints: The Root CA is the highest authority in the PKI hierarchy. It is self-signed and signs Intermediate CA certificates.
topics.narration:
Root CA

The Root CA is the highest authority in the PKI hierarchy. It is self-signed and signs Intermediate CA certificates.

Important exam points. The Root CA is the highest authority in the PKI hierarchy. It is self-signed and signs Intermediate CA certificates.. Self-Signed. Usually kept Offline. Highest Trust

topics.title: Intermediate CA
topics.definition: An Intermediate CA receives trust from the Root CA and issues certificates to servers, users, and devices.
examPoints: An Intermediate CA receives trust from the Root CA and issues certificates to servers, users, and devices.
topics.narration:
Intermediate CA

An Intermediate CA receives trust from the Root CA and issues certificates to servers, users, and devices.

Important exam points. An Intermediate CA receives trust from the Root CA and issues certificates to servers, users, and devices.. Root CA. ↓. ↓. End Entity Certificate

topics.title: Why Intermediate CA?
topics.narration:
Why Intermediate CA?

Important exam points. If Intermediate is compromised,. Root remains safe.

topics.title: Registration Authority (RA)
topics.definition: The Registration Authority verifies the identity of certificate applicants before the CA issues certificates.
examPoints: The Registration Authority verifies the identity of certificate applicants before the CA issues certificates.
topics.narration:
Registration Authority (RA)

The Registration Authority verifies the identity of certificate applicants before the CA issues certificates.

Important exam points. The Registration Authority verifies the identity of certificate applicants before the CA issues certificates.. RA. Verifies Identity. CA. Issues Certificate

topics.title: RA vs CA
comparisons: RA: Identity Verification; CA: Certificate Issuance
RA: Doesn't Sign; CA: Signs Certificates
topics.narration:
RA vs CA

Comparison. RA: Identity Verification; CA: Certificate Issuance. RA: Doesn't Sign; CA: Signs Certificates

topics.title: Repository
topics.definition: A PKI Repository stores certificates, CRLs, CA certificates, and related PKI information.
examPoints: A PKI Repository stores certificates, CRLs, CA certificates, and related PKI information.
topics.narration:
Repository

A PKI Repository stores certificates, CRLs, CA certificates, and related PKI information.

Important exam points. A PKI Repository stores certificates, CRLs, CA certificates, and related PKI information.. Stores. Certificates. CRLs. CA Certificates. LDAP. HTTP

topics.title: Validation Authority (VA)
topics.definition: A Validation Authority checks whether certificates are valid or revoked.
examPoints: A Validation Authority checks whether certificates are valid or revoked.
topics.narration:
Validation Authority (VA)

A Validation Authority checks whether certificates are valid or revoked.

Important exam points. A Validation Authority checks whether certificates are valid or revoked.. Uses. CRL. OCSP

topics.title: Subscriber
topics.definition: The Subscriber is the entity that owns the certificate and private key.
examPoints: The Subscriber is the entity that owns the certificate and private key.
topics.narration:
Subscriber

The Subscriber is the entity that owns the certificate and private key.

Important exam points. The Subscriber is the entity that owns the certificate and private key.. User. Server. Device

topics.title: Relying Party
topics.definition: A Relying Party is the entity that verifies and trusts certificates issued by the PKI.
examPoints: A Relying Party is the entity that verifies and trusts certificates issued by the PKI.
topics.narration:
Relying Party

A Relying Party is the entity that verifies and trusts certificates issued by the PKI.

Important exam points. A Relying Party is the entity that verifies and trusts certificates issued by the PKI.. Browser validating HTTPS Certificate

topics.title: Trust Model
topics.definition: A Trust Model defines how trust relationships are established between Certificate Authorities.
examPoints: A Trust Model defines how trust relationships are established between Certificate Authorities.
topics.narration:
Trust Model

A Trust Model defines how trust relationships are established between Certificate Authorities.

Important exam points. A Trust Model defines how trust relationships are established between Certificate Authorities.. Types. Hierarchical. Mesh. Bridge. Web of Trust

topics.title: Hierarchical Trust Model
topics.definition: One Root CA signs one or more Intermediate CAs, which issue certificates.
examPoints: One Root CA signs one or more Intermediate CAs, which issue certificates.
topics.narration:
Hierarchical Trust Model

One Root CA signs one or more Intermediate CAs, which issue certificates.

Important exam points. One Root CA signs one or more Intermediate CAs, which issue certificates.. Most Common. Internet uses this

topics.title: Mesh Trust Model
topics.definition: Every CA directly trusts every other CA.
examPoints: Every CA directly trusts every other CA.
topics.narration:
Mesh Trust Model

Every CA directly trusts every other CA.

Important exam points. Every CA directly trusts every other CA.. Complex. Not scalable

topics.title: Bridge CA
topics.definition: A Bridge CA connects multiple independent PKIs without replacing existing Root CAs.
examPoints: A Bridge CA connects multiple independent PKIs without replacing existing Root CAs.
topics.narration:
Bridge CA

A Bridge CA connects multiple independent PKIs without replacing existing Root CAs.

Important exam points. A Bridge CA connects multiple independent PKIs without replacing existing Root CAs.. Used in Large Enterprises

topics.title: Web of Trust
topics.definition: Users trust each other by signing each other's public keys.
examPoints: Users trust each other by signing each other's public keys.
topics.narration:
Web of Trust

Users trust each other by signing each other's public keys.

Important exam points. Users trust each other by signing each other's public keys.. Used by. PGP. GPG

topics.title: Cross Certification
topics.definition: Two independent CAs mutually trust each other by exchanging certificates.
examPoints: Two independent CAs mutually trust each other by exchanging certificates.
topics.narration:
Cross Certification

Two independent CAs mutually trust each other by exchanging certificates.

Important exam points. Two independent CAs mutually trust each other by exchanging certificates.. CA A. ↔. CA B

topics.title: Certificate Policy (CP)
topics.definition: A Certificate Policy defines the rules governing certificate issuance, management, and usage.
examPoints: A Certificate Policy defines the rules governing certificate issuance, management, and usage.
topics.narration:
Certificate Policy (CP)

A Certificate Policy defines the rules governing certificate issuance, management, and usage.

Important exam points. A Certificate Policy defines the rules governing certificate issuance, management, and usage.. Defines. Identity Requirements. Algorithms. Key Sizes. Validity

topics.title: Certificate Practice Statement (CPS)
topics.definition: A CPS explains how a CA actually implements the Certificate Policy.
examPoints: A CPS explains how a CA actually implements the Certificate Policy.
topics.narration:
Certificate Practice Statement (CPS)

A CPS explains how a CA actually implements the Certificate Policy.

Important exam points. A CPS explains how a CA actually implements the Certificate Policy.. CP = Rules. CPS = Implementation

topics.title: OID (Object Identifier)
topics.definition: An OID uniquely identifies certificate policies, extensions, algorithms, and PKI objects.
examPoints: An OID uniquely identifies certificate policies, extensions, algorithms, and PKI objects.
Every Certificate Policy has an OID.
topics.narration:
OID (Object Identifier)

An OID uniquely identifies certificate policies, extensions, algorithms, and PKI objects.

Important exam points. An OID uniquely identifies certificate policies, extensions, algorithms, and PKI objects.. Every Certificate Policy has an OID.

topics.title: Enterprise PKI
topics.definition: Enterprise PKI manages certificates for employees, servers, applications, devices, VPNs, Wi-Fi, and internal services.
examPoints: Enterprise PKI manages certificates for employees, servers, applications, devices, VPNs, Wi-Fi, and internal services.
topics.narration:
Enterprise PKI

Enterprise PKI manages certificates for employees, servers, applications, devices, VPNs, Wi-Fi, and internal services.

Important exam points. Enterprise PKI manages certificates for employees, servers, applications, devices, VPNs, Wi-Fi, and internal services.. Active Directory Certificate Services. Microsoft PKI. EJBCA

topics.title: Complete PKI Workflow
workflows: Key Pair Generated ↓ CSR Created ↓ RA Verifies Identity ↓ CA Signs Certificate ↓ Certificate Issued ↓ Certificate Installed ↓ Client Connects ↓ Certificate Verified ↓ Secure Communication
topics.narration:
Complete PKI Workflow

Workflow. Key Pair Generated ↓ CSR Created ↓ RA Verifies Identity ↓ CA Signs Certificate ↓ Certificate Issued ↓ Certificate Installed ↓ Client Connects ↓ Certificate Verified ↓ Secure Communication

topics.title: Certificate Lifecycle (PKI View)
workflows: Generate Keys ↓ CSR ↓ Verification ↓ Issuance ↓ Distribution ↓ Installation ↓ Renewal ↓ Rekey ↓ Revocation ↓ Expiration ↓ Destruction
topics.narration:
Certificate Lifecycle (PKI View)

Workflow. Generate Keys ↓ CSR ↓ Verification ↓ Issuance ↓ Distribution ↓ Installation ↓ Renewal ↓ Rekey ↓ Revocation ↓ Expiration ↓ Destruction

topics.title: CA vs RA
comparisons: CA: Signs Certificates; RA: Verifies Identity
CA: Trusted Authority; RA: Registration Authority
topics.narration:
CA vs RA

Comparison. CA: Signs Certificates; RA: Verifies Identity. CA: Trusted Authority; RA: Registration Authority

topics.title: Root CA vs Intermediate CA
comparisons: Root: Self-Signed; Intermediate: Signed by Root
Root: Highest Trust; Intermediate: Issues Certificates
topics.narration:
Root CA vs Intermediate CA

Comparison. Root: Self-Signed; Intermediate: Signed by Root. Root: Offline; Intermediate: Online. Root: Highest Trust; Intermediate: Issues Certificates

topics.title: Subscriber vs Relying Party
comparisons: Subscriber: Owns Certificate; Relying Party: Verifies Certificate
topics.narration:
Subscriber vs Relying Party

Comparison. Subscriber: Owns Certificate; Relying Party: Verifies Certificate

topics.title: Repository vs Validation Authority
comparisons: Repository: Stores Certificates; Validation Authority: Validates Certificates
topics.narration:
Repository vs Validation Authority

Comparison. Repository: Stores Certificates; Validation Authority: Validates Certificates. Repository: LDAP/HTTP; Validation Authority: CRL/OCSP

topics.title: Top 25 One-Liners for Exam
oneLiners: PKI manages digital certificates and trust.
PKI uses asymmetric cryptography.
CA issues and signs certificates.
RA verifies identity.
Repository stores certificates and CRLs.
VA validates certificate status.
Subscriber owns the certificate.
Relying Party verifies the certificate.
Root CA is self-signed.
Root CA is usually kept offline.
Intermediate CA issues end-user certificates.
Internet mainly uses a Hierarchical Trust Model.
PGP uses a Web of Trust.
Bridge CA connects separate PKIs.
Cross Certification allows two CAs to trust each other.
Certificate Policy defines what must be followed.
CPS defines how the policy is implemented.
Every Certificate Policy has an OID.
Browsers trust certificates because the Root CA certificate exists in the trust store.
CA trust is inherited through the certificate chain.
End-entity certificates should not be signed directly by the Root CA.
PKI solves the public key authentication problem.
Digital certificates bind an identity to a public key.
Without PKI, anyone could claim any public key belongs to them.
The most important PKI workflow to remember is: Key Pair → CSR → RA Verification → CA Signing → Certificate → Validation → Secure Communication.
topics.narration:
Top 25 One-Liners for Exam

Cross Certification allows two CAs to trust each other.. Certificate Policy defines what must be followed.. CPS defines how the policy is implemented.. Every Certificate Policy has an OID.. Browsers trust certificates because the Root CA certificate exists in the trust store.. CA trust is inherited through the certificate chain.. End-entity certificates should not be signed directly by the Root CA.. PKI solves the public key authentication problem.. Digital certificates bind an identity to a public key.. Without PKI, anyone could claim any public key belongs to them.. The most important PKI workflow to remember is: Key Pair → CSR → RA Verification → CA Signing → Certificate → Validation → Secure Communication.
